items.title: 1.2 Commercial Driver License (CDL) and Commercial Learner Permit (CLP)
U.S. Department
items.body:
U.S. Department 
of Transportation
Federal Motor 
Carrier Safety 
Administration
Entry-Level Driver Training Requirements for CDL Applicants
The new Entry-Level Driver Training (ELTD) regulations require that all entry-level drivers of commercial motor vehicles 
(CMVs) receive training from a provider listed on FMCSA's Training Provider Registry.
Beginning February 7, 2022, to be eligible to take required skills or knowledge tests, commercial driver's license (CDL) 
applicants must have completed applicable entry-level driver training from a registered training provider.
Use the scenario chart below to determine if you are required to complete entry-level driver training. 
If >Then
A driver holds a CLP that was 
issued before February 7, 2022...
The driver is not required to complete entry-level 
driver training, as long as the driver obtains a CDL 
before the CLP expires.
A driver was issued a CDL or an 
S, P, or H endorsement before February 7, 2022...
The driver is not required to complete entry, level 
driver training for the previously-issued license or 
endorsement, even if it has since lapsed.
A driver holds a CLP that was issued before 
February 7, 2022, but the CLP expires before the 
driver applies for his or her CDL...
The driver must complete the required entry level 
driver training.
A driver obtains a CLP on or after February 7, 2022...
The driver must complete the required entry-level 
driver training.
A driver held a CDL prior to the compliance date 
and applies for an upgrade to a higher class 
of CDL, or an S, P, or H endorsement for the first 
time on Or after February 7, 2022...
The driver must complete the required entry-level 
driver training for the class of CDL to which the driver 
is upgrading or the endorsement(s) for which the 
driver is applying.
ELDT EXCEPTIONS. The ELDT regulations do not apply to individuals that are not required to have a CDL as 
outlined in 49 CFR part 383, or for whom the state has waived the CDL skills test.
What must drivers do to meet the ELDT requirements?
Complete training with a registered training provider
Entry-level drivers must select a training provider that is listed on the 
Training Provider Registry. Only registered training providers will be 
able to submit certification of a driver's completion energy-level driver 
training to the Training Provider Registry.
When am I eligible to take my CDL skills or knowledge test? 
Training must be completed before taking a CDL skills test or, if the driver 
is applying for the H endorsement, the knowledge test.
If the State Driver Licensing Agency cannot electronically verify that these 
requirements are met, the State is not permitted to administer the CDL skills 
or knowledge test to the driver.
LEARN MORE AT: 
http://tpr.fmcsa.dot.gov

items.title: SECTION 1
items.body:
SECTION 1 
INTRODUCTION 
This Section Covers 
•
• Commercial Motor Vehicles (CMV) 
•
Commercial Driver License (CDL) and Commercial Learner Permit (CLP) Classes,
 Endorsements & Restrictions 
• •Commercial Driver License and Commercial Learner Permit Requirements 
• Commercial Driver License Tests 
•
Additional Requirements For Bus Drivers 
• Driver Disqualifications 
• Other Commercial Privilege Rules 
•
International Registration Plan and International Fuel Tax Agreement

items.title: 1.1 - Commercial Motor Vehicles (CMV)
items.body:
1.1 - Commercial Motor Vehicles (CMV) 
You must have a Commercial Driver License (CDL) or Commercial Learner Permit (CLP) to operate 
 any of the following CMVs: 
•••
Any single vehicle with a gross vehicle weight rating (GVWR) of 26,001 pounds or more. 
•••
A vehicle towing a trailer with a GVWR of more than 10,000 pounds if the gross combination weight 
rating (GCWR) is 26,001 pounds or more. GCWR is the GVWR of the power unit plus the GVWR of 
 each trailer in combination. 
•••
A vehicle designed to transport 15 or more passengers (excluding the driver) or a vehicle 
defined as a bus under Article 19-A, Section 509-a of the NYS Vehicle and Traffic Law (VTL). 
••• An altered vehicle commonly referred to as a “stretch limousine” that has been modified to carry 9 or 
more passengers including the driver. 
•••
Any size vehicle that requires hazardous materials placards or is carrying material listed as a select 
agent or toxin in 42 CFR part 73. Federal regulations through the Department of Homeland 
 Security require a background check and fingerprinting for the Hazardous Materials endorsement. 
EXEMPTIONS 
Drivers of the following vehicles that otherwise meet the definition of a CMV are exempt from the 
 CDL requirement: 
•••
A vehicle owned and controlled by a farmer that has a GVWR of more than 26,000 lbs and is 
used to transport agricultural products, farm machinery, or farm products within 150 miles of the farm. 
•••
Off-road motorized construction equipment such as motor scrapers, backhoes, motor graders, 
 compactors, tractors, trenchers, bulldozers and railroad track maintenance cranes are exempt when 
operated at a construction site not accessible to the public or when operated on a public road open 
to unrestricted public travel for limited distances at less than normal highway speeds, provided the 
equipment is not used in furtherance of a transportation purpose. Such vehicles, when occasionally 
driven on a public road to reach or leave a construction site must be accompanied by escort vehicles 
or in some other way separated from the public traffic. 
•••
Fire and police vehicles engaged in emergency operations in New York State. 
•••
Military vehicles or combination of vehicles operated by members of the armed forces. 
•••
Personal vehicles (including rental vehicles up to 26,000 lbs GVWR) when operated strictly and 
 exclusively to transport personal possessions or family members for non-commercial purposes. 
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10
 
Page 
1-1 
 (2/26)

items.title: 1.2 - Commercial Driver License (CDL) and Commercial Learner Permit (CLP) Classes,
items.body:
1.2 - Commercial Driver License (CDL) and Commercial Learner Permit (CLP) Classes, 
Endorsements & Restrictions 
CDL and CLP classes, endorsements and restrictions are based on the type of CMV driven. The type of 
CMV is determined by the vehicle manufacturer's GVWR (for single vehicles) or GCWR (for combination 
vehicles), construction or use. (Under the NYS VTL, CMV weight classifications are based on the
 greater of the following weights: manufacturer's GVWR or GCWR, registration weight, or actual weight 
of the vehicle(s) and load.) CLP/CDL classes, endorsements and restrictions, therefore, correspond to 
vehicle weight, construction or use, as shown in Figure 1.1, 1.2 and 1.3 on page 1-2.

items.title: 1.3 – Commercial Driver License and Commercial Learner Permit Requirements
items.body:
1.3 – Commercial Driver License and Commercial Learner Permit Requirements 
New York State driver licensing standards comply with the federal laws related to commercial 
driver licenses (CDL) and commercial learner permits (CLP). To get a NYS CDL or CLP you must 
 meet the following standards and requirements:

items.title: 3.1 – Legal Presence Requirement
items.body:
3.1 – Legal Presence Requirement 
US citizens and Lawful Permanent Residents must provide proof of citizenship or unrestricted 
Lawful Permanent Residency at a DMV office. Once you have met this requirement in a DMV 
office you will not be required to prove your legal status again. Applicants from a foreign 
jurisdiction must provide proof of your legal presence at a DMV office for every transaction. 
Please refer to form ID-44CDL for a list of all the documents that you may submit as proof of US 
 citizenship, Lawful Permanent Residency, or temporary legal presence.

items.title: 3.2 – Residency Requirement
items.body:
3.2 – Residency Requirement 
You must be a resident of New York State to be issued a NYS CLP or CDL. One proof of 
residence is required to apply, transfer, renew, and amend your CLP/CDL. Please refer to form 
ID-44CDL for a list of all the documents that you may submit as proof of New York State residency. 
A driver holding a CDL issued by another jurisdiction who moves to New York must apply for a 
NYS CDL within 30 days after establishing residency. A new resident may apply to exchange a 
 CDL issued by another jurisdiction for a NYS CDL (reciprocity). However, to keep a Hazardous 
Material (HazMat) endorsement, you must pay the test fee, take and pass the HazMat written 
knowledge test (a score of 80% is passing), and pay for and pass a background investigation. 
 (See Section 9, Hazardous Materials.)

items.title: 3.3 – Age Requirement
items.body:
3.3 – Age Requirement
 Class A, B and C – You must be at least 18 years of age, but if under 21, you can drive a CMV 
 only for intrastate commerce (within New York State), cannot transport students in a school bus, 
 and cannot transport hazardous material.

items.title: 3.4 – Language Requirement
items.body:
3.4 – Language Requirement 
You must be able to read and speak the English language well enough to: 
•••••• converse with other people, 
••••••
understand highway traffic signs and signals in the English language, 
•••••• answer questions from officials, 
••••••
make entries on reports and records, and 
••••••
take skills tests as required for getting your CDL 
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10
 
Page 
1-3 
 (2/26)

items.title: 3.5 – Self-Certification Requirement
items.body:
3.5 – Self-Certification Requirement
 Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) regulations require all New York State CLP 
or CDL holders to certify with the Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV) to the type of commercial 
operation they perform or expect to perform. All CLP or CDL holders must certify to one of the 
 four categories listed in Figure 1-4, (Self-Certification Driving Types), prior to the issuance, amendment, 
or renewal of a CLP or CDL. In addition, depending upon which driving type you self-certify to, 
you may have certain medical requirements that apply to you. An explanation of medical 
requirements can be found in section 1.3.6. 
NON-EXCEPTED INTERSTATE (NI) 
• Must be age 21 or over 
• Must not have a K or A3 restriction 
• CDL holders with a Medical Variance 
must have the V restriction 
• CDL holders with a H or X

items.title: 1.4 Self-certification driving types
items.body:
1.4 Self-certification driving types
 “Interstate” commerce is when you drive a CMV: 
• From one state to another or a foreign country; 
• Between two places within New York State but, during part of the trip, the CMV crosses into 
 another state or foreign country; or 
• Between two places within New York State, but the cargo is part of a trip that began or will end 
 in another state or foreign country. 
“Intrastate” commerce is when you drive a CMV only within New York State and you do not meet 
any of the descriptions above for interstate commerce. 
Page 1-4 
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10
 
 (2/26)

items.title: 3.6 – Medical Examination Requirement
items.body:
3.6 – Medical Examination Requirement 
If you self-certify to a Non-Excepted (NA or NI) driving type, the federal government requires you 
to have a medical examination in order to detect physical or mental conditions that may affect 
your ability to operate a motor vehicle safely. The examination requirements are found in the U.S.
 Department of Transportation’s (USDOT) Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration Regulations 
under 49 CFR Part 391. The USDOT medical exam covers 13 areas that directly relate to the 
 ability to drive safely. You must pass the USDOT medical examination, and be medically 
 certified to self-certify to a Non-Excepted driving type. 
If your Medical Examiner’s Certificate indicates that it must be accompanied by a 
waiver/exemption or Skills Performance Evaluation (SPE) Certificate, a valid FMCSA 
variance of the same type indicated on your medical certificate must be on file with the 
 DMV. 
Your CLP or CDL will not be issued, renewed, or amended if you do not have a valid medical 
certificate on file. 
Note: The physical examination conducted just for bus drivers who are subject to Article 
19-A of the NYS Vehicle and Traffic Law does not meet the requirements of Part 391 of the 
 Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations, unless the federal medical standards, 
 procedures and forms were used. 
••••••
HOW LONG IS THE MEDICAL EXAMINER’S CERTIFICATE VALID? A USDOT medical exam 
 is normally valid for 24 months. However, your medical examiner may give you a medical 
 examiner’s certificate that expires in less than 24 months in order to monitor a condition, such 
as high blood pressure, which the examiner wants to check more often than every two years. 
••••••
WHO MAY PERFORM THE MEDICAL EXAM? Only doctors listed on the FMCSA National 
 Registry of Certified Medical Examiners may perform a USDOT medical exam. 
••••••
HOW DO I FIND A LICENSED MEDICAL EXAMINER? To find a certified doctor in your area, 
 please visit the Federal website at: 
 http://www.fmcsa.dot.gov/regulations/national-registry/national-registry-certified-medical-examiners 
•

items.title: 3.7 – Medical Examination Exception
Controlled and operated by a farmer, but is not a combination vehicle (power unit and
items.body:
• Controlled and operated by a farmer, but is not a combination vehicle (power unit and 
 towed unit), and is used to transport agricultural products, farm machinery or farm supplies 
 (no placardable hazardous materials) to and from a farm and within 150 air-miles of the farm; 
• As a private motor carrier of passengers for non-business purposes; 
• To transport migrant workers, must be age 21 or older. 
• Drivers who obtained their NYS CDL prior to 9/9/1999 are permitted to perform “non-
 excepted” commercial operation (except when transporting hazardous materials) in NYS 
 only .These drivers must self-certify to EA.

items.title: 3.8 – Application and Written Testing Requirements
items.body:
3.8 – Application and Written Testing Requirements 
To get a Class A, B, or C CDL for the first time, to upgrade to a higher Class CDL, or to add a “P” 
or “S” endorsement to a CDL, you must first obtain a Commercial Learner Permit (CLP) for the 
proper class and type of vehicle you plan to drive. To obtain the learner permit, you must already 
have a NYS driver license that is not suspended, revoked or cancelled. You must apply in person 
at a NYS motor vehicles office*. Allow three hours to complete all of the processing, 
including test taking. Plan to arrive no later than three hours before the office’s scheduled closing 
 time. At the motor vehicles office you will: 
••••••
Complete an application form (MV-44), in which you must: 
••••• provide proof of your legal presence in the U.S. (see form ID-44CDL) 
••••• provide proof of your NYS residence (see form ID-44CDL); 
••••• list all the states (including the District of Columbia) in which you were licensed to drive 
over the past 10 years; 
••••• certify to the type of commercial operation you perform, or expect to perform; 
••••••
Show your NYS driver license. 
•••••• Show your Social Security Card, if NYS DMV has no record of your Social Security Number and 
you are asked to show your card. 
••••••
Pay the fees that cover the application, processing the photo document, and testing. 
••••••
Pass the general knowledge test and any necessary endorsement tests by answering at least 
 80% of the questions correctly (see section 1.4); you are not allowed to bring anything into the 
testing room or area except a pencil, and you must present your driver license again before you 
can take the test(s). Reminder: cell phones are not permitted in any test area at any time. 
••••••
Federally mandated restrictions on CLPS: 
••••• CLPs issued with a Passenger (P) or School Bus (S) endorsement will automatically get a 
new federally mandated “No Passengers in CMV Bus” (P) restriction. 
••••• CLPs issued with a Tank Vehicles (N) endorsement will automatically get a federally 
 mandated “No Cargo in CMV Tank Vehicle” (X) restriction. 
••••• The P and X restrictions are issued on the CLP only. The CDL that is issued after all 
required tests have been passed will not have the P or X restrictions. 
*Locations and hours of NYS Motor Vehicles offices may be found in your local telephone 
directory, at the NYS DMV web site at dmv.ny.gov/offices.htm, or by calling NYS DMV, weekdays 
 (except state holidays) between 8:00 am and 4:30 pm (Eastern Time) at 1-800-698-2931.

items.title: 3.9 – Driving With Your Commercial Learner Permit
items.body:
3.9 – Driving With Your Commercial Learner Permit 
Your commercial driver learner permit allows you to drive a vehicle of the class and type that 
 matches the class and any endorsements of your permit, as long as: 
••••••
your CLP is accompanied by an underlying NYS license. 
Page 1-6 
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10 (2/26)

items.title: Reference / Front Matter
items.body:
••••••
a driver holding a CDL of the same or higher class with the proper endorsements accompanies 
you at all times; 
••••••
your permit and the supervising driver’s CDL do not have any restrictions that would prohibit 
 driving in the practice vehicle; and 
•••••• you are not transporting any material that requires hazardous material (HazMat) placards or any 
quantity of material listed as a selected agent or toxin in 42 CFR 73, even when the supervising 
 driver’s CDL has a HazMat endorsement or you yourself hold a lower class of CDL with a 
 HazMat endorsement.

items.title: 3.10 – Skills Testing Requirements
items.body:
3.10 – Skills Testing Requirements 
To get your CDL you must pass the skills test in a representative vehicle – one that matches the 
class, type and use of the CDL you need. Study sections 11, 12 and 13 in this manual for details 
about the CDL skills test, and practice driving with your learner permit as much as possible until 
you are confident that you could pass the test. You may schedule your skills test either on-line
 at dmv.ny.gov or by telephone at 1-518-402-2100. You must wait a minimum of 14 days from the 
date you obtained your CLP before you may take the skills test. It normally takes several weeks 
from the date that you make your appointment to the date of the test, so schedule your test early. 
You may also cancel a scheduled skills test on-line or by telephone, but you must cancel your 
appointment no less than three full business days prior to your scheduled road test date and time. If 
you cancel later than that you will lose the skills test fee, and must pay the skills test fee again 
before rescheduling. Additional information for scheduling a skills test can be found on the DMV 
 website mentioned above. 
To pass the skills test you must demonstrate that you can inspect and operate the representative 
vehicle safely and competently, with 50 or fewer points deducted. All individuals who pass a road 
test will be instructed to wait 1 day before going to DMV to receive their permanent CDL license. 
During the test, if you have or cause a crash, commit any single traffic violation or dangerous 
action, or lose more than 50 points, you will fail the skills test. If you fail a skills test you may 
schedule an appointment for the next test date, based on appointment availability. There is no 
minimum waiting period before retaking the skills test (except that you may take only one test per 
day), but the next appointment date may be several weeks from the date you make it. You must 
pay the skills test fee again before you can schedule your next appointment.

items.title: 3.11 – Military Waiver of Skills Test
items.body:
3.11 – Military Waiver of Skills Test 
The Regulations of the Commissioner of Motor Vehicles allow a CDL applicant with military 
experience driving commercial motor vehicles to apply for a waiver of the CDL skills test. 
 To meet the requirements for this waiver, the applicant must: 
•
Currently hold a valid NYS driver license or have a valid out-of-state driver license to be 
 exchanged for a NYS driver license 
•
Meet all other requirements to qualify for a NYS CDL 
•
Be currently regularly employed (or regularly employed within the last 12 months) in a military 
 position (active duty or New York National Guard) that requires operation of a CMV 
• Have operated a CMV of the type that the driver operates (or expects to operate) for at least 
 two years immediately before: 
• The application date, if in active service, or 
• The date of being discharged from the military 
At a DMV office, the applicant must present: 
••••••
A completed CDL Certification for Military Waiver of Skills Test (form CDL-102), available at 
 dmv.ny.gov/forms/CDL102.pdf or any Motor Vehicles office 
••••••
A valid NYS or out-of-state driver license, 
•
Proof of military service as listed on form CDL-102 
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10
 
Page 
1-7 
 (2/26)

items.title: You must pass all applicable written tests and pay the written test fee, permit fee and license fee.
items.body:
You must pass all applicable written tests and pay the written test fee, permit fee and license fee. 
 There is no skills test fee. 
If all requirements are met, the DMV office will issue your CDL. The class, endorsements and 
restrictions on your CDL will depend on the type of CMV you operated in the military. For more 
information see the Explanation of License Classes, Endorsements and Restrictions (form MV-500C). 
1.3.12– FMCSA School Bus Only Exemption (School Bus Only Restriction) 
The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) offers an exemption of certain 
pre-trip vehicle inspection skills test requirements for certain school bus drivers. 
The FMCSA exemption waives the engine compartment component of the pre-trip vehicle inspection 
skills testing requirement, also known as the “under-the-hood” component, for new CDL applicants 
or applicants with a previous CDL who want the School Bus (S) and Passenger (P) endorsements. 
Applicants who have a current CDL are not eligible for the FMCSA exemption. 
CDL applicants seeking the FMCSA exemption must take the “Limited School Bus” road test, 
and the “K” Intrastate Only and “S1” School Bus Only restrictions must be applied. 
This exemption may expire. Applicants must pass the “Limited School Bus” road test and visit a DMV 
office on or before the expiration date to amend their license to a CDL. After the FMCSA exemption 
expires, a CDL with the “S1” School Bus Only restriction cannot be renewed. To maintain a 
CDL, drivers must apply for a permit, take a full CDL road test, and pay all required fees. 
Drivers who want to remove the “K” Intrastate Only and “S1” School Bus Only restrictions must 
Page 1-8 
apply for a permit, take a full CDL road test in a vehicle representative of the restriction being 
removed, and pay all required fees. 
 
 More information about the exemption and its expiration date can be found on DMV’s website. 
 
 1.3.13– FMCSA Transit Bus Only Exemption (Transit Bus Only Restriction) 
 
 The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) offers an exemption of certain pre-trip 
 vehicle inspection skills test requirements for certain transit bus drivers. 
 
 The FMCSA exemption waives the engine compartment component of the pre-trip vehicle 
 inspection skills testing requirement, also known as the “under-the-hood” component, for new 
 CDL applicants or applicants with a previous CDL who want the Passenger (P) endorsement. 
 Applicants who have a current CDL are not eligible for the FMCSA exemption. 
 
 CDL applicants seeking the FMCSA exemption must take the “Limited Transit Bus” road test, and 
 the “K” Intrastate Only and “T1” Transit Bus Only restrictions must be applied. 
 
 This exemption may expire. Applicants must pass the “Limited Transit Bus” road test and visit a 
 DMV office on or before the expiration date to amend their license to a CDL. After the FMCSA 
 exemption expires, a CDL with the “T1” Transit Bus Only restriction cannot be renewed. To 
 maintain a CDL, drivers must apply for a permit, take a full CDL road test, and pay all required 
 fees. 
 
 Drivers who want to remove the “K” Intrastate Only and “T1” Transit Bus Only restrictions must 
 apply for a permit, take a full CDL road test in a vehicle representative of the restriction being 
 removed, and pay all required fees. 
 
 More information about the exemption and its expiration date can be found on DMV’s website. 
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10 (2/26)

items.title: Reference / Front Matter
items.body:
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10
 
Page 
1-9 
 1.3.14– Alcohol and Drug Testing Requirements 
 
 Under New York State law, any person who operates a motor vehicle in New York is deemed to 
 have given consent to a chemical test of one or more of the following: breath, blood, urine or 
 saliva, for the purpose of determining the alcoholic and/or drug content of the blood.

items.title: 3.15 - Drug and Alcohol Clearing House (DACH)
items.body:
3.15 - Drug and Alcohol Clearing House (DACH) 
 
 Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) regulations require all New York CLP or 
 CDL applicants or holders to be listed as “Not Prohibited” from performing safety-sensitive 
 functions, including driving a commercial motor vehicle (CMV), due to one or more drug and 
 alcohol program violations. New York State may not issue, renew, upgrade, or transfer a CDL, or 
 CLP, as applicable, for any individual prohibited under FMCSA’s regulations from performing 
 safety-sensitive functions. The Clearinghouse is a secure online database that gives employers, 
 FMCSA, state driver licensing agencies, and state law enforcement personnel real-time 
 information about CDL and CLP holders’ drug and alcohol program violations.

items.title: 1.4 – Commercial Driver License Tests
items.body:
1.4 – Commercial Driver License Tests 
 
 To get a CDL, you must pass knowledge and skills tests. The sole purpose of this manual is to help 
 you pass the tests. This manual is not a substitute for a commercial driver training class or program. 
 Formal training is the most reliable way to learn the many special skills required for safely driving a 
 large commercial vehicle and becoming a professional commercial driver. Section 1.4.1, shows the 
 sections of this manual you should study for each particular class of license and for each endorsement.

items.title: 4.1 – Knowledge Tests
items.body:
4.1 – Knowledge Tests 
 
 You will have to take one or more knowledge tests, depending on what class of license and what 
 endorsements you need. All test questions are multiple-choice; you must choose one correct 
 answer from three choices. The passing score for each commercial driver knowledge test is 80%. 
 If you fail a knowledge test there is no limit to the number of times you can retake it, however, this 
 will be at the discretion of the District Director, Office Manager, County Clerk or Office Supervisor. 
 
 l The General (Core) Knowledge Test is taken by all CLP or CDL applicants. For this test, study 
 
 Sections 1, 2, 3, 11, 12, and 13 of this manual. 
 
 When taking any other CDL knowledge test, all test-takers should study Sections 1, 2, 3, 
 11, 12, and 13 in addition to the section(s) listed below. The other CDL knowledge tests, and 
 the additional sections of this manual that you should study to pass each of them, are: 
 l The Combination Vehicles Test, required to drive combination vehicles (Class A CDL); Study 
 Sections 5 and 6 
 
 l The Doubles/Triples Test, required to pull double or triple trailers; Study Sections 5, 6, and 7 
 
 l The Hazardous Materials Test, required to haul hazardous materials or waste in amounts that 
 require placarding or any quantity of a material listed as a select agent or toxin in 42 CFR 73. 
 In order to obtain this endorsement, you are also required to pass both a New York State and 
 a Transportation Security Administration (TSA) background check; Study l The Passenger Transport Test, taken by all bus driver applicants; Study l The School Bus Test, required to drive a school bus with a GVWR of more than 26,000 lbs or 
 designed to transport 15 or more passengers (excluding the driver); Study Section 10 
 l The Tank Vehicles Test, required to haul a liquid or liquid gas in a permanently mounted cargo 
 tank rated at 119 gallons or more or a portable tank rated at 1,000 gallons or more; Study 
 Sections 6, 8, and 9 
 
 l The Air Brakes Test, which you must take if your vehicle has air brakes, including air-over-
 hydraulic brakes; Study Section 5
 (2/26)

items.title: 4.2 – Skills Test
items.body:
4.2 – Skills Test 
 
 If you pass the required knowledge test(s), you can take the CDL skills test. You must wait a 
 minimum of 14 days from the date you obtained your CLP before you may take the skills test. 
 There are three types of general skills that will be tested: pre-trip vehicle inspection, basic vehicle 
 control, and on-road driving. You must take the skills test in the class and type of vehicle for which 
 you wish to be licensed. Any vehicle that has marked or labeled components cannot be used for the 
 pre-trip vehicle inspection test. 
 
 Pre-trip Vehicle Inspection. You will be tested to see if you know whether your vehicle is safe to 
 drive. The pre-trip vehicle inspection must be passed before you can proceed to the basic vehicle 
 control skills test. You will be asked to do a pre-trip inspection of your vehicle and explain to the 
 examiner what you would inspect and why. See Section 11 for details. 
 
 Basic Vehicle Control. You will be tested on your skill to control the vehicle. You will be asked to 
 move your vehicle forward, backward, and turn it within a defined area. These areas may or may 
 not be marked with traffic lanes, cones, barriers, or something similar. The examiner will tell you 
 how each control test is to be done. See Section 12 for details. 
 
 On-road Driving Test. You will be tested on your skill to safely drive your vehicle in a variety of 
 traffic situations. The situations may include left and right turns, intersections, railroad crossings, 
 curves, up and down grades, single or multi-lane roads, streets, or highways. The examiner will 
 tell you where to drive. See Section 13 for details.

items.title: 1.5 – Additional Requirements For Bus Drivers
items.body:
1.5 – Additional Requirements For Bus Drivers 
 
 All bus drivers in New York State must have CDLs, and employers must determine that the drivers 
 they hire are qualified to drive buses. Furthermore, Article 19-A (Special Requirements For Bus 
 Drivers) of the NYS VTL sets standards for bus drivers. Under this law, employers of bus drivers must: 
 
 l Conduct a background investigation of a new driver’s employment history for the past three years. 
 
 l Obtain driving records from all jurisdictions where the driver worked, lived or had a driver license 
 or learner permit in the past 3 years. 
 
 l Tell drivers about the provisions of Article 19-A. 
 
 l Require that drivers take an initial physical examination and then follow-up exams every two years. 
 
 l Require that every driver with a current or past history of diabetes obtain an initial diabetic 
 certification and then follow up certification every six months. 
 
 l Annually review the driving record of each driver to determine if he/she meets the minimum 
 requirements to drive a bus. 
 
 l Annually observe each driver’s defensive driving performance while operating a bus carrying 
 passengers. 
 
 l Give each driver a written or oral examination every two years to test his/her knowledge of the rules 
 of the road, defensive driving practices and laws regulating bus driving in New York State. 
 
 l Give each driver a behind-the-wheel driving test every two years. 
 
 l Subject a driver who fails to provide notification within 30 days of the date of hire and then every 
 two years of convictions and accidents to a five working-day suspension, or, if the conviction is for 
 a misdemeanor or felony, a suspension equivalent to the number of working days a driver was not 
 in compliance with the reporting requirements, or five working days, whichever is longer. 
 
 Additional Requirements For School Bus Drivers. Article 19-A requires that employers of school bus 
 drivers request a criminal history check on each driver based on his/her fingerprints. NYS 
 Education Department (NYSED) regulation further requires that drivers of school buses: 
 
 l Be at least 21 years of age. 
 
 l Take and pass a yearly follow-up physical exam. 
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10 (2/26)

items.title: Reference / Front Matter
items.body:
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10
 
Page 1-11 
 If a bus driver fails to meet any of the legal or regulatory requirements, the employer must not allow 
 the driver to operate a bus until requirements are met. The Department of Motor Vehicles also 
 disqualifies drivers based on their driver record, criminal history, and compliance with Article 19-A.

items.title: 6.1 – General
items.body:
6.1 – General 
 
 You may not drive a commercial motor vehicle if you are disqualified for any reason.

items.title: 6.2 – Alcohol, Leaving the Scene of an Accident, and Commission of a Felony
items.body:
6.2 – Alcohol, Leaving the Scene of an Accident, and Commission of a Felony 
 
 It is illegal to operate a CMV if your blood alcohol concentration (BAC) is .04% or more. If you 
 operate a CMV, you shall be deemed to have given your consent to alcohol testing. 
 You will be put out-of-service for 24 hours if you have any detectable amount of alcohol under .04%. 
 
 You will lose your CLP and/or CDL for at least one year for a first offense for: 
 
 l Driving a CMV if your blood alcohol concentration is .04% or higher. 
 
 l Driving any vehicle under the influence of alcohol. 
 
 l Driving any vehicle while under the influence of a controlled substance. 
 
 l Refusing to undergo blood alcohol testing. 
 
 l Leaving the scene of an accident without reporting. 
 
 l Committing a felony involving the use of a vehicle. 
 
 l Operating a CMV while your CLP and/or CDL is revoked, suspended, or canceled for prior 
 violations, or if disqualified from operating a CMV, or if convicted for causing a fatality through 
 negligent operation of a CMV, including, but not limited to crimes of vehicular manslaughter or 
 criminally negligent homicide. 
 
 You will lose your CLP and/or CDL for at least three years if the offense occurs while you are 
 operating a CMV that is placarded for hazardous materials. 
 
 You will lose your CLP and/or CDL for life if convicted a second time for any of the offenses listed above. 
 
 You will lose your CLP and/or CDL for life if you use a CMV to commit a felony involving controlled 
 substances.

items.title: 6.3 – Serious Traffic Violations
items.body:
6.3 – Serious Traffic Violations 
 
 Serious traffic violations include: 
 
 l Excessive speeding (15 mph or more above the posted limit) 
 
 l Reckless driving 
 
 l Improper or erratic lane changes 
 
 l Following a vehicle too closely 
 
 l Traffic offenses committed in a CMV in connection with fatal traffic accidents 
 
 l Operating a CMV without first obtaining a CLP or CDL 
 
 l Operating a CMV without a CLP or CDL in the driver’s possession 
 
 l Operating a CMV without the proper class of CLP or CDL and/or endorsement for the specific 
 vehicle being operated or for the passengers or type of cargo being transported 
 l Operating a CMV while using a handheld cell phone and/or texting device. 
 (2/26)

items.title: Page 1-12
items.body:
Page 1-12 
 You will lose your CLP and/or CDL: 
 
 l For at least 60 days if you have committed two serious traffic violations within a three-year 
 period involving a CMV. 
 
 l For at least 120 days for three serious traffic violations within a three-year period involving a CMV.

items.title: 6.4 – Violation of Out-of-Service Orders
items.body:
6.4 – Violation of Out-of-Service Orders 
 
 If a federal or state inspector judges you or your commercial vehicle to be unsafe during an 
 inspection, they will order you or your vehicle out-of-service. 
 You will lose your CLP and/or CDL if convicted of operating in violation of the out-of-service order: 
 
 l For at least 90 days if you have committed your first violation of an out-of-service order. 
 
 l For at least one year if you have committed two violations of out-of-service orders within a 
 ten-year period. 
 
 l For at least three years if you have committed three or more violations of out-of-service orders 
 within a ten-year period.

items.title: 6.5 – Railroad-Highway Grade Crossing Violations
items.body:
6.5 – Railroad-Highway Grade Crossing Violations 
 
 These violations, while operating a commercial motor vehicle, include violation of a federal, state 
 or local law or regulation pertaining to one of the following six offenses at a railroad-highway 
 grade crossing: 
 
 l For drivers who are not required to always stop, failing to stop before reaching the crossing if 
 the tracks are not clear. 
 
 l For drivers who are not required to always stop, failing to slow down and check that the tracks 
 are clear of an approaching train. 
 
 l For drivers who are always required to stop, failing to stop before driving onto the crossing. 
 
 l For all drivers failing to have sufficient space to drive completely through the crossing 
 without stopping. 
 
 l For all drivers failing to obey a traffic control device or the directions of an enforcement official 
 at the crossing. 
 
 l For all drivers failing to negotiate a crossing because of insufficient undercarriage clearance. 
 
 You will lose your CLP and/or CDL: 
 
 l For at least 60 days for your first violation. 
 
 l For at least 120 days for your second violation within a three-year period. 
 
 l For at least one year for your third violation within a three-year period.

items.title: 6.6 – Hazardous Materials Endorsement Background Check and Disqualifications
items.body:
6.6 – Hazardous Materials Endorsement Background Check and Disqualifications 
 
 If you require a hazardous materials endorsement you will be required to submit your fingerprints 
 and be subject to a background check. 
 
 You will be denied or you will lose your hazardous materials endorsement if you: 
 
 l Are not a lawful permanent resident of the United States. 
 
 l Renounce your United States citizenship. 
 
 l Are wanted or under indictment for certain felonies. 
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10 (2/26)

items.title: Reference / Front Matter
items.body:
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10
 
Page 1-13 
 
 l Have a conviction in military or civilian court for certain felonies. 
 
 l Have been adjudicated as lacking mental capacity or are involuntarily committed to a mental 
 health facility as specified in 49 CFR 1572.109. 
 
 l Are considered to pose a security threat as determined by the Transportation 
 Security Administration. 
 
 Additional information is available at: dmv.ny.gov/cdl.htm

items.title: 6.7 – Traffic Violations in Your Personal Vehicle
items.body:
6.7 – Traffic Violations in Your Personal Vehicle 
 
 Federal and State rules require a CLP and CDL holder to be disqualified from operating a 
 commercial motor vehicle if the CLP or CDL holder has been convicted of certain types of moving 
 violations in their personal vehicle. This includes: leaving the scene of an accident, violations 
 involving alcohol and/or drugs, and felonies involving a motor vehicle. 
 
 If your privilege to operate your personal vehicle is revoked, cancelled, or suspended due to 
 violations of traffic control laws (other than parking violations) you will also lose your commercial 
 driving privileges. 
 
 If your privilege to operate your personal vehicle is revoked, cancelled, or suspended due to 
 alcohol, controlled substance, or felony violations, you will lose your CLP and/or CDL for 1 year. 
 If you are convicted of a second such violation in your personal vehicle or CMV, you will lose your
 CLP or CDL for life. 
 
 If your license to operate your personal vehicle is revoked, cancelled, or suspended, you may 
 not obtain a “hardship” license to operate a CMV.

items.title: 1.7 – Other Commercial Privilege Rules
items.body:
1.7 – Other Commercial Privilege Rules 
 
 There are other federal and state rules that affect drivers operating CMVs in all states. Among them are:

items.title: 7.1 – Licensing Rules
items.body:
7.1 – Licensing Rules 
 
 l No one can drive a commercial motor vehicle without a CLP or CDL. A court may fine you 
 $75-$300 or put you in jail for breaking this rule. 
 
 l You cannot have more than one license. If you break this rule, a court may fine you $75-$300
 or put you in jail and keep your home state license and return any others. 
 
 l If you have a hazardous materials endorsement, you must notify and surrender your hazardous 
 materials endorsement to the state that issued your CDL within 24 hours if: 
 
 Ø you are convicted, indicted, or found not guilty by reason of insanity in any jurisdiction, 
 civilian or military, for a disqualifying crime listed in 49 CFR 1572.103; 
 
 Ø you are adjudicated as lacking mental capacity or are involuntarily committed to a mental 
 health facility as specified in 49 CFR 1572.109; or 
 
 Ø you renounce your U.S. citizenship. 
 
 l All states are connected to one computerized system to share information about CLP and CDL 
 drivers. The states will check driving records and make sure that drivers do not have more than 
 one CLP and CDL. 
 
 l You must be properly restrained by a safety belt at all times while operating a commercial motor 
 vehicle. The safety belt design holds the driver securely behind the wheel during a crash, helping 
 the driver to control the vehicle, and reduces the chance of serious injury or death. If you do not wear 
 a safety belt, you are four times more likely to be fatally injured if you are thrown from the vehicle. 
 (2/26)

items.title: 7.2 – Employment Rules
items.body:
7.2 – Employment Rules 
 
 l You must give your employer information on all driving jobs you have held for the past 10 years. 
 You must do this when you apply for a commercial driving job. 
 
 l You must notify your employer within 30 days of conviction for any traffic violations (except 
 parking). This is true no matter what type of vehicle you were driving. 
 
 l You must notify your employer if your license is suspended, revoked, or canceled, or if you are 
 disqualified from driving. 
 
 l Your employer may not let you drive a commercial motor vehicle if you have more than one 
 license or if your CLP or CDL is suspended, revoked or cancelled. A court may fine the employer 
 up to $5,000 or put him/her in jail for breaking this rule.

items.title: 1.8 – International Registration Plan and International Fuel Tax Agreement
items.body:
1.8 – International Registration Plan and International Fuel Tax Agreement 
 
 If you operate a CMV in interstate commerce, the vehicle, with few exceptions, is required to be 
 registered under the International Registration Plan (IRP) and the International Fuel Tax Agreement 
 (IFTA). These programs provide for the collection and distribution of vehicle registration fees and fuel 
 use taxes for interstate travel.

items.title: 8.1 - International Registration Plan (IRP) administered by the Department of
items.body:
8.1 - International Registration Plan (IRP) administered by the Department of 
 Motor Vehicles (DMV) 
 
 Under the IRP, jurisdictions must register vehicles and audit registrant mileage records. 
 Registrants must apply for IRP registration, provide proper documentation, pay appropriate fees, 
 properly display credentials, maintain accurate and compliant mileage records, and make records 
 available for audit.

items.title: 8.2 - International Fuel Tax Agreement (IFTA) administered by the Department of
items.body:
8.2 - International Fuel Tax Agreement (IFTA) administered by the Department of 
 Taxation and Finance (DTF) 
 
 IFTA enables interstate carriers to report fuel used and pay fuel use taxes. 
 
 Under the IFTA, a carrier is issued one set of credentials to travel in all IFTA member jurisdictions. 
 The fuel use taxes collected pursuant to the IFTA are calculated based on the number of miles/ 
 kilometers traveled and the number of gallons/liters consumed in all member jurisdictions. The base 
 jurisdiction will collect and distribute fuel use taxes to other member jurisdictions and 
 perform fuel use tax audits. 
 
 Carriers must file IFTA quarterly tax returns with the base jurisdiction to report travel and fuel use
 
 in all IFTA member jurisdictions. Carriers must retain mileage and fuel records to support their 
 IFTA quarterly tax returns.

items.title: 8.3 – Additional Information and Recordkeeping Requirements
items.body:
8.3 – Additional Information and Recordkeeping Requirements 
 
 Additional information and recordkeeping requirements for the IRP can be obtained from DMV’s IRP 
 Instruction Manual (IRP-8), available under “Business Use Forms” at dmv.ny.gov. IRP, Inc. is the 
 official repository for the IRP, and additional information can be found on their website at www.irponline.org 
 There is a training video on their website available in English, Spanish and French. 
 
 For additional information on IFTA, visit the Department of Taxation and Finance website at
 http://www.tax.ny.gov/. You will also find useful information at the official IFTA repository at 
 http://www.iftach.org/index.php

items.title: 8.4 - Record Retention for IRP and IFTA
items.body:
8.4 - Record Retention for IRP and IFTA 
 
 For IRP, mileage records supporting IRP registrations must be retained for 6 years. 
 
 For IFTA, mileage and fuel records supporting IFTA quarterly fuel use tax returns must 
 be retained for 4 years.
 
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10 (2/26)

items.title: SECTION 2
items.body:
SECTION 2 
DRIVING SAFELY 
 
This Section Covers 
 
l Vehicle Inspection l Driving in Winter
l Basic Control of Your Vehicle l Driving in Very Hot Weather 
l Shifting Gears l Railroad-Highway Crossings 
l Seeing l Mountain Driving 
l Communicating l Driving Emergencies 
l Controlling Speed l Antilock Braking Systems (ABS) 
l Managing Space l Skid Control and Recovery 
l Seeing Hazards l Crash Procedures 
l Distracted Driving l Fires 
l Aggressive Drivers/Road Rage l Alcohol, Other Drugs, and Driving 
l Driving at Night l Staying Alert and Fit to Drive 
l Driving in Fog l Hazardous Materials Rules for All 
 Commercial Drivers 
 
 This section contains knowledge and safe driving information that all commercial drivers should 
 know. You must pass a test on this information to get a CDL. This section does not have specific 
 information on air brakes, combination vehicles, doubles, or passenger vehicles. When preparing for 
 the Pre-trip Inspection Test, you must review the material in Section 11 in addition to the information 
 in this section. This section does have basic information on hazardous materials (HazMat) that all 
 drivers should know. If you need a HazMat endorsement, you should study Section 9.

items.title: 1.1 – Why Inspect
items.body:
1.1 – Why Inspect 
 
 Safety is the most important reason you inspect your vehicle, safety for yourself and for other road users. 
 
 A vehicle defect found during an inspection could save you problems later. You could have a 
 breakdown on the road that will cost time and dollars, or even worse, a crash caused by the defect. 
 
 Federal and state laws require that drivers inspect their vehicles. Federal and state inspectors 
 also may inspect your vehicles. If they judge the vehicle to be unsafe, they will put it "out of 
 service" until it is fixed.

items.title: 1.2 – Types of Vehicle Inspection
items.body:
1.2 – Types of Vehicle Inspection 
 
 Pre-trip Inspection. A pre-trip inspection will help you find problems that could cause a crash or 
 breakdown. 
 
 During a Trip. For safety you should: 
 
 l Watch gauges for signs of trouble. 
 
 l Use your senses to check for problems (look, listen, smell, feel). 
 
 l Check critical items when you stop: 
 
 Ø Tires, wheels and rims. 
 Ø Brakes. 
 Ø Lights and reflectors. 
 Ø Brake and electrical connections to trailer. 
 Ø Trailer coupling devices. 
 Ø Cargo securement devices. 
2-1
Page
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10 (11/25)

items.title: STEERING SYSTEM
items.body:
STEERING SYSTEM 
 After-trip Inspection and Report. You should do an after-trip inspection at the end of the trip, day, or 
 tour of duty on each vehicle you operated. It may include filling out a vehicle condition report listing any 
 problems you find. The inspection report helps a motor carrier know when the vehicle needs repairs.

items.title: 1.3 – What to Look For
items.body:
1.3 – What to Look For 
 
 Tire Problems 
 
 l Too much or too little air pressure. 
 
 l Bad wear. You need at least 4/32-inch tread depth in every major groove on front tires. You need 
 2/32 inch on other tires. No fabric should show through the tread or sidewall. 
 
 l Cuts or other damage. 
 
 l Tread separation. 
 
 l Dual tires that come in contact with each other or parts of the vehicle. 
 
 l Mismatched sizes. 
 
 l Radial and bias-ply tires used together. 
 
 l Cut or cracked valve stems. 
 
 l Regrooved, recapped, or retreaded tires on the front wheels of a bus. These are prohibited. 
 
 Wheel and Rim Problems 
 
 l Damaged rims. 
 
 l Rust around wheel nuts may mean the nuts are loose--check tightness. After a tire has been 
 changed, stop a short while later and re-check tightness of nuts. 
 
 l Missing clamps, spacers, studs, or lugs means danger. 
 
 l Mismatched, bent, or cracked lock rings are dangerous. 
 
 l Wheels or rims that have had welding repairs are not safe. 
 
 Bad Brake Drums or Shoes 
 
 l Cracked drums. 
 
 l Shoes or pads with oil, grease, or brake fluid 
 on them. 
 
 l Shoes worn dangerously thin, missing, 
 or broken. 
 
 Steering System Defects 
 
 l Missing nuts, bolts, cotter keys, or other parts. 
 
 l Bent, loose, or broken parts, such as steering 
 column, steering gear box, or tie rods. 
 
 l If power steering equipped, check hoses, 
 pumps, and fluid level; check for leaks. 
 
 l Steering wheel play of more than 10 degrees 
 (approximately 2 inches movement at the rim 
 of a 20-inch steering wheel) can make it hard 
 to steer. 
 
 Figure 2.1 illustrates a typical steering system. 
2-2
Page
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10 (11/25)
Figure 2.1

items.title: AIR SUSPENSION PARTS
items.body:
AIR SUSPENSION PARTS 
BROKEN LEAF IN SPRING 
SAFETY DEFECT: 
KEY SUSPENSION PARTS 
 Suspension System Defects. The suspension system holds up the vehicle and its load. It keeps 
 the axles in place. Therefore, broken suspension parts can be extremely dangerous. Look for: 
 
 l Spring hangers that allow movement of axle 
 from proper position. See Figure 2.2. 
 
 l Cracked or broken spring hangers. 
 
 l Missing or broken leaves in any leaf spring 
 (see Figure 2.3). Any defect could be dangerous. 
 If any of the following conditions exist the vehicle 
 will be placed “out of service”, and must be repaired: 
 
 1) One-fourth or more of the leaves in any 
 spring assembly are broken 
 
 2) Any leaf or portion of any leaf in any 
 spring assembly is missing or separated 
 
 3) Any main leaf in a leaf spring is broken. 
 
 l Broken leaves in a multi-leaf spring or leaves that 
 have shifted so they might hit a tire or other part. 
 
 l Leaking shock absorbers. 
 
 l Torque rod or arm, u-bolts, spring hangers, or 
 other axle positioning parts that are cracked, 
 damaged, or missing. 
 
 l Air suspension systems that are damaged 
 and/or leaking. See Figure 2.4. 
 
 l Any loose, cracked, broken, or missing 
 frame members. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Exhaust System Defects. A broken exhaust system can let poison fumes into the cab or sleeper berth. 
Look for: 
 
 l Loose, broken, or missing exhaust pipes, mufflers, tailpipes, or vertical stacks. 
 
 l Loose, broken, or missing mounting brackets, clamps, bolts, or nuts. 
 
 l Exhaust system parts rubbing against fuel system parts, tires, or other moving parts of vehicle. 
 
 l Exhaust system parts that are leaking. 
2-3
Page
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10 (11/25)
Figure

items.title: Emergency Equipment. Vehicles must be equipped with emergency equipment. Look for:
items.body:
Emergency Equipment. Vehicles must be equipped with emergency equipment. Look for: 
 
 l Fire extinguisher(s). 
 
 l Spare electrical fuses (unless equipped with circuit breakers). 
 
 l Warning devices for parked vehicles (for example, three reflective warning triangles). 
 
 Cargo (Trucks). You must make sure the truck is not overloaded and the cargo is balanced and 
 secured before each trip. If the cargo contains hazardous materials, you must inspect for proper 
 papers and placarding.

items.title: 1.4 – CDL Pre-trip Vehicle Inspection Test
items.body:
1.4 – CDL Pre-trip Vehicle Inspection Test 
 
 In order to obtain a CDL you will be required to pass a skills test that includes a pre-trip vehicle 
 inspection. You will be tested to see if you know whether your vehicle is safe to drive. You will be 
 asked to do a pre-trip inspection of your vehicle and explain to the examiner what you would 
 inspect and why. The following seven-step inspection method should be useful.

items.title: 1.5 – Seven-step Inspection Method
items.body:
1.5 – Seven-step Inspection Method 
 
 Method of Inspection. You should do a pre-trip inspection the same way each time so you will 
 learn all the steps and be less likely to forget something. 
 
 Approaching the Vehicle. Notice general condition. Look for damage or vehicle leaning to one 
 side. Look under the vehicle for fresh oil, coolant, grease, or fuel leaks. Check the area around 
 the vehicle for hazards to vehicle movement (people, other vehicles, objects, low-hanging wires, 
 limbs, etc.). 
 
 Vehicle Inspection Guide 
 
 Step 1: Vehicle Overview 
 
 Review Last Vehicle Inspection Report. Drivers may have to make a vehicle inspection report 
 in writing each day. The motor carrier must repair any items in the report that affect safety and 
 certify on the report that repairs were made or were unnecessary. You must sign the report only if 
 defects were noted and certified to be repaired or not needed to be repaired. 
 
 Step 2: Check Engine Compartment 
 
 Check That the Parking Brakes Are On and/or Wheels Chocked. You may have to raise the 
 hood, tilt the cab (secure loose things so they don't fall and break something), or open the engine 
 compartment door. Check the following: 
 
 l Engine oil level. 
 
 l Coolant level in radiator; condition of hoses. 
 
 l Power steering fluid level; hose condition (if so equipped). 
 
 l Windshield washer fluid level. 
 
 l Battery fluid level, connections, and tie downs (battery may be located elsewhere). 
 
 l Automatic transmission fluid level (may require engine to be running). 
 
 l Check belts for tightness and excessive wear (alternator, water pump, air compressor)--learn 
 how much "give" the belts should have when adjusted right, and check each one. 
 
 l Leaks in the engine compartment (fuel, coolant, oil, power steering fluid, hydraulic fluid, 
 battery fluid). 
 
 l Cracked, worn electrical wiring insulation. 
 
 Lower and secure hood, cab, or engine compartment door. 
2-4
Page
New York State Commercial Driver’s Manual CDL-10 (11/25)

items.title: Step 3: Start Engine and Inspect Inside the Cab
items.body:
Step 3: Start Engine and Inspect Inside the Cab 
 
 Get In and Start Engine 
 
 l Make sure parking brake is on. 
 
 l Put gearshift in neutral (or "park" if automatic). 
 
 l Start engine; listen for unusual noises. 
 
 l If equipped, check the Anti-Lock Braking System (ABS) indicator lights. Light on dash should 
 come on and then go off. If it stays on, the ABS is not working properly. For trailers only, if the 
 yellow light on the left rear of the trailer stays on, the ABS is not working properly. 
 
 Look at the Gauges 
 
 l Oil pressure. Should come up to normal within seconds after engine is started. See Figure 2.5 
 
 l Air pressure. Pressure should build from 50 to 90 psi within 3 minutes. Build air pressure to 
 governor cut-out (usually around 120-140 psi; know your vehicle’s requirements). 
 
 l Ammeter and/or voltmeter. Should be in normal range(s). 
 
 l Coolant temperature. Should begin gradual rise to normal operating range. 
 
 l Engine oil temperature. Should begin gradual rise to normal operating range. 
 
 l Warning lights and buzzers. Oil, coolant, charging circuit warning, and antilock brake system 
 lights should go out right away. 
 
 Check Condition of Controls. Check all of the following for looseness, sticking, damage, or 
 improper setting: 
 
 l Steering wheel. l Interaxle differential lock (if vehicle has one). 
 
 l Clutch. l Horn(s). 
 
 l Accelerator ("gas pedal"). l Windshield wiper/washer. 
 
 l Brake controls. l Lights. 
 Ø Foot brake. Ø
Headlights. 
 Ø Trailer brake (if vehicle has one). Ø
Dimmer switch. 
 Ø Parking brake. Ø
Turn signal. 
 Ø Retarder controls (if vehicle has them). Ø
Four-way flashers. 
 
 l Transmission controls. Ø
Parking, clearance, identification, 
 
 marker switch(es).
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Check Mirrors and Windshield. Inspect mirrors and windshield for cracks, dirt, illegal stickers, or other 
obstructions to seeing clearly. Clean and adjust as necessary.

items.title: Check Emergency Equipment
items.body:
Check Emergency Equipment 
 
 l Check for safety equipment: 
 
 Ø Spare electrical fuses (unless vehicle has circuit breakers). 
 Ø Three red reflective triangles. 
 Ø Properly charged and rated fire extinguisher. 
 
 l Check for optional items such as: 
 
 Ø Chains (where winter conditions require). 
 Ø Tire changing equipment. 
 
 l List of emergency phone numbers. 
 
 l Accident reporting kit (packet). 
 
 Check Safety Belt 
 
 l Check that the safety belt is securely mounted, adjusts, latches properly and is not ripped or frayed. 
 
 Step 4: Turn Off Engine and Check Lights 
 
 Make sure the parking brake is set, turn off the engine, and take the key with you. Turn on 
 headlights (low beams) and four-way emergency flashers, and get out of the vehicle. 
 
 Step 5: Do Walk-Around Inspection 
 
 l Go to front of vehicle and check that low beams are on and both of the four-way flashers 
 are working. 
 
 l Push dimmer switch and check that high beams work. 
 
 l Turn off headlights and four-way emergency flashers. 
 
 l Turn on parking, clearance, side-marker, and identification lights. 
 
 l Turn on right turn signal, and start walk-around inspection. 
 
 General 
 
 l Walk around and inspect. 
 
 l Clean all lights, reflectors, and glass as you go along. 
 
 Left Front Side 
 
 l Driver's door glass should be clean. 
 
 l Door latches or locks should work properly. 
 
 l Left front wheel. 
 
 Ø Condition of wheel and rim – no missing, bent, or broken studs, clamps, or lugs, or any 
 signs of misalignment. 
 Ø Condition of tires - properly inflated, valve stem and cap OK, no serious cuts, bulges, or 
 tread wear. 
 Ø Use wrench to test rust-streaked lug nuts, indicating looseness. 
 Ø Hub oil level OK, no leaks. 
 
 l Left front suspension. 
 
 Ø Condition of spring, spring hangers, shackles, u-bolts. 
 Ø Shock absorber condition.

items.body:
l Left front brake. 
 
 Ø Condition of brake drum or disc.

 Front 
 
 l Condition of front axle. 
 
 l Condition of steering system. 
 
 Ø No loose, worn, bent, damaged or missing parts. 
 Ø Must grab steering mechanism to test for looseness. 
 
 l Condition of windshield. 
 Ø Check for damage and clean if dirty. 
 Ø Check windshield wiper arms for proper spring tension. 
 Ø Check wiper blades for damage, "stiff" rubber, and secure attachment. 
 
 l Lights and reflectors. 
 
 Ø Parking, clearance, and identification lights clean, operating, and proper color 
 (amber at front). 
 Ø Reflectors clean and proper color (amber at front). 
 Ø Right front turn signal light clean, operating, and proper color (amber or white on signals 
 facing forward). 
 
 Right Side 
 
 l Right front: check all items as done on left front. 
 
 l Primary and secondary safety cab locks engaged (if cab-over-engine design). 
 
 l Right fuel tank(s). 
 
 Ø Securely mounted, not damaged, or leaking. 
 Ø Fuel crossover line secure. 
 Ø Tank(s) contain enough fuel. 
 Ø Cap(s) on and secure. 
 
 l Condition of visible parts. 
 
 Ø Rear of engine--not leaking. 
 Ø Transmission--not leaking. 
 Ø Exhaust system--secure, not leaking, not touching wires, fuel, or air lines. 
 Ø Frame and cross members--no bends or cracks. 
 Ø Air lines and electrical wiring--secured against snagging, rubbing, wearing. 
 Ø Spare tire carrier or rack not damaged (if so equipped). 
 Ø Spare tire and/or wheel securely mounted in rack. 
 Ø Spare tire and wheel adequate (proper size, properly inflated). 
 
 l Cargo securement (trucks). 
 Ø Cargo properly blocked, braced, tied, chained, etc. 
 Ø Header board adequate, secure (if required). 
 Ø Side boards, stakes strong enough, free of damage, properly set in place (if so equipped). 
 Ø Canvas or tarp (if required) properly secured to prevent tearing, billowing, or blocking 
 of mirrors. 
 Ø If oversize, all required signs (flags, lamps, and reflectors) safely and properly mounted 
 and all required permits in driver's possession. 
 Ø Curbside cargo compartment doors in good condition, securely closed, latched/locked and 
 required security seals in place.

items.title: Right Rear
items.body:
Right Rear 
 
 l Condition of wheels and rims--no missing, bent, or broken spacers, studs, clamps, or lugs. 
 
 l Condition of tires--properly inflated, valve stems and caps OK, no serious cuts, bulges, tread 
 wear, tires not rubbing each other, and nothing stuck between them. 
 
 l Tires same type, e.g., not mixed radial and bias types. 
 
 l Tires evenly matched (same sizes). 
 
 l Wheel bearing/seals not leaking. 
 
 l Suspension. 
 
 Ø Condition of spring(s), spring hangers, shackles, and u-bolts. 
 Ø Axle secure. 
 Ø Powered axle(s) not leaking lube (gear oil). 
 Ø Condition of torque rod arms, bushings. 
 Ø Condition of shock absorber(s). 
 Ø If retractable axle equipped, check condition of lift mechanism. If air powered, check 
 for leaks. 
 Ø Condition of air ride components. 
 
 l Brakes. 
 
 Ø Brake adjustment. 
 Ø Condition of brake drum(s) or discs. 
 Ø Condition of hoses--look for any wear due to rubbing. 
 
 l Lights and reflectors. 
 
 Ø Side-marker lights clean, operating, and proper color (red at rear, others amber). 
 Ø Side-marker reflectors clean and proper color (red at rear, others amber). 
 
 Rear 
 
 l Lights and reflectors. 
 
 Ø Rear clearance and identification lights clean, operating, and proper color (red at rear). 
 Ø Reflectors clean and proper color (red at rear). 
 Ø Taillights clean, operating, and proper color (red at rear). 
 Ø Right rear turn signal operating, and proper color (red, yellow, or amber at rear). 
 
 l License plate(s) present, clean, and secured. 
 
 l Splash guards present, not damaged, properly fastened, not dragging on ground, or 
 rubbing tires. 
 
 l Cargo secure (trucks). 
 
 l Cargo properly blocked, braced, tied, chained, etc. 
 
 Ø Tailboards up and properly secured. 
 Ø End gates free of damage, properly secured in stake sockets. 
 Ø Canvas or tarp (if required) properly secured to prevent tearing, billowing, or blocking of 
 either the rearview mirrors or rear lights. 
 Ø If over-length, or over-width, make sure all signs and/or additional lights/flags are safely 
 and properly mounted and all required permits are in driver's possession. 
 Ø Rear doors securely closed, latched/locked. 
 
 Left Side 
 
 l Check all items as done on right rear and right side, plus:
